zotero.abstractNote: Object-centric learning (OCL) aims to decompose scenes into individual objects, promising better generalization, interpretability, and data efficiency. Despite years of research, OCL models still struggle to scale to complex, real-world data. In this paper, we perform a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art OCL models on a diverse set of benchmarks. We find that while OCL models perform well on synthetic datasets, their performance drops significantly on real-world data. We identify key bottlenecks in current OCL architectures and propose a new framework, OCCAM, that addresses these limitations. Our results show that OCCAM achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, suggesting that we are not done with OCL, but rather that we need to rethink our approach to scaling it.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Workshop on Spurious Correlation and Shortcut Learning: Foundations and Solutions (ICLR 2025)
